canonical_definition: Across the surveyed literature, PAWS is predominantly used as a benchmark to measure `Paraphrase identification`. It is defined as a "paraphrase detection benchmark" that evaluates whether two sentences are semantically equivalent, specifically assessing a model's performance in distinguishing paraphrases from non-paraphrases. While its most common application is for paraphrase-related tasks, one paper also uses PAWS to measure `Safety`. The benchmark serves as a downstream dataset for quantitative evaluation, with one study reporting "particularly notable gains of 3.17% and 12.38% in PAWS" to demonstrate the effectiveness of its methods against a conventional approach.
papers: An Efficient Task-Oriented Dialogue Policy: Evolutionary Reinforcement Learning Injected by Elite Individuals
Generating Plausible Distractors for Multiple-Choice Questions via Student Choice Prediction
OmniCharacter: Towards Immersive Role-Playing Agents with Seamless Speech-Language Personality Interaction
The MaleCEOand the Female Assistant: Evaluation and Mitigation of Gender Biases in Text-To-Image Generation of Dual Subjects
